title: Staining Experiment and Sorting of 293F Cells
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"p4p1","text":"This protocol details the staining and sorting of 293F cells. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"11juu","text":"Staining is a time-intensive process and is benefitted from doing in small batches rather than many samples at once, since cells are out of their incubator growing environment for \u003e4 hours. Booking the TC hood for a long time will be necessary.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"18lrn","text":"Experiments take 3 days to prepare, so plan accordingly and schedule flow time ahead of plating the experiment.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"duive","text":"If sorting cells (rather than just analysis), be prepared to re-plate sorted cells immediately after flow cytometry.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6immv","text":"Wipe down with 70% ethanol and/or Envirocide when entering/exiting the tissue culture hood","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2lmnp","text":"Staining cells is a bit of an art and can take time to master. Start with small experiments and then gradually move upwards.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c5bg5","text":"Wash steps will be identical throughout, unless annotated otherwise. Staining/blocking buffer is PBS + 1% BSA for most antibodies, but is a 1:10 dilution of PBS + Ca2+ + Mg2+ into PBS, then adding 1% BSA. This is because Ca2+ is required for carboxylation-sensitive antibody binding, but high levels of Ca2+ are toxic to these cells. I found that this dilution of commercially-available PBS + Ca2+ + Mg2+ gives good staining and minimal cell toxicity.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Count the cell line(s) desired for use in staining protocol using a hemocytometer or cell counter and trypan blue.
Wash with 1 mL blocking buffer per sample, pipetting gently up and down 3-4 times to mix.
Aspirate off media, being careful to not disturb the pellet It’s fine to leave a little media at the bottom to not disturb the pellet
For each sample to be stained, take 4 mL of cell culture media and put it into a labeled flow cytometry tube.
Two days after plating, proceed with staining cells.
The day after plating, add 10 µL - 30 µL doxycycline ( 2 mg/ml stock, final concentration 2 μg/ml ) to each flask.
If assaying carboxylation with GMA-001 or ab3570, add 10 µL warfarin ( 100 millimolar (mM) stock, final concentration 100 micromolar (µM) ) to one of the two WT flasks as a negative control.
Add 10 µL - 30 µL vitamin K1 ( 50 millimolar (mM) stock, final concentration 50 nanomolar (nM) ) to each experimental flask.
Passage the cells. Be sure to keep/passage at least one maintenance flask using standard procedures if you will be using these cells again at another point in the near future.
Spin 300 x g, °C, 3 minutes
Take cells to flow cytometry for analysis and/or sorting.
After 30 minutes, add 1 mL of wash buffer and wash cells. Spin at 300 x g, °C, 3 minutes and aspirate off the wash buffer. Repeat this step 2 more times (3 total washes).

Incubate each sample in 100 µL diluted secondary antibody for 00:30:00 at Room temperature in the dark. Pipette up and down gently 3-4 times to mix. Every 10 minutes, briefly vortex cells to ensure even mixing and staining of cells.
Dilute secondary antibody(ies) 1:100 – 1:1000 in wash buffer based on dilutions listed in the Antibodies section. You will need 100 µL of diluted antibody per sample you plan to stain.
After 30 minutes, add 1 mL of wash buffer and wash cells. Spin at 300 x g, °C, 3 minutes and aspirate off the wash buffer. Repeat this step 2 more times (3 total washes).

Incubate each sample in 100 µL diluted primary antibody for 00:30:00 at Room temperature . Pipette up and down gently 3-4 times to mix. Every 10 minutes, briefly vortex cells to ensure even mixing and staining of cells.

Dilute primary antibody(ies) 1:100 – 1:1000 in wash buffer based on dilutions listed in the Antibodies section. You will need 100 µL of diluted antibody per sample you plan to stain. If you are testing a new antibody, start at 1:100 dilution and titrate upwards and downwards to find the best separation of positive and negative cells. Include an unstained control at this point to determine if there is background staining in your negative control wells.
Spin at 300 x g, °C, 3 minutes , aspirate off the wash buffer, and repeat once more (3 total washes).
Resuspend cells in 500 µL wash buffer and pipette up and down gently 3-4 times to mix. If you are staining libraries, combine your tubes into 2 flow tubes of ~ 3 mL stained cells each.

Prepare several (5-8) sets of 4 recovery tubes for the sorted cells. Label each set with numbers 1-4 to represent the bins that the sorter will separate cells into. Add 2 mL Freestyle 293 media to each recovery tube.
Install the recovery tubes in the sorter tray with Bin 1 on the left and Bin 4 on the right.
Create 4 gates on the GFP:mCherry graph - they should each contain ~25% of the total population shown on the graph. Label them Bin 1-4 from left to right.
Load a library tube in the sample holder and record data for this tube.
Record data for all controls before beginning sorting.

Create a graph from the BFP-negative, mCherry-positive cells that shows the ratio of GFP to mCherry on the x-axis and select “histogram” on the y-axis. This graph will set the gates for sorting the cells.

Create a gate for the BFP-negative and mCherry-positive cells (in the top-left corner of the graph). This will be the sorted population. Note the percentage of the single cell population that expresses mCherry. Click on “Record data”.

Create a graph that shows BFP fluorescence on the x-axis and mCherry on the y-axis. Adjust the BFP laser setting if needed so that the main population (with strong BFP fluorescence) is centered over 10 5 . Adjust the mCherry laser setting if needed so that the main population (high mCherry fluorescence) is centered over 10 5 on the y-axis.

Gate for single cells by gating in the following order: FSC-A vs SSC-A to gate for live cells FSC-A vs FSC-H to gate for single cells in the forward direction SSC-A vs SSC-H to gate for single cells in the side direction
Remove the cap and load the flow tube containing cells into the sample loader. Press “Load” on the software. Analysis should begin automatically, or press “Acquire data”.
On the flow cytometer software, open your experiment and create tubes within that experiment for the controls and variant library.
Click on the “Sort” button in the sort layout window.
Choose the stall which contains the recovery tube for Bin 1, and select the gated population labeled as Bin 1 to sort into the recovery tube. Repeat for the remaining 3 bins.
In the software’s experiment dashboard, open the “Cytometer settings”, then open a sort layout.

Resuspend the cells from each Bin in a total of 1 mL Freestyle 293 media. Ex: Combine all recovery tubes from Bin 1 by resuspending in 1 mL divided between the recovery tubes and combining them in one tube after resuspension.
Aspirate the media from the recovery tubes, being very careful not to disturb the pellet.
Return the sorted cells to the tissue culture room. Centrifuge the recovery tubes at 300 x g, °C, 5 minutes . Look for a small cell pellet at the bottom of the tubes.
Remove tubes from the cytometer and follow instructions for post-sorting cleanup given by the manager of the flow cytometer.
Sort cells into the prepared recovery tubes until the total number of cells needed is reached.

As the flow cytometer sorts the cells, pay attention to the volumes in both the original flow tube and the recovery tube. Do NOT let the original flow tube run empty, or the recovery tube overflow. To stop the sorter, click on “Pause” in the sort layout window - this does not stop the sorter from pulling from the original flow tube. Click on “Stop Acquisition” or “Unload” if it is necessary to change the tube on the sample loader. Record the number of cells sorted into each Bin for each set of recovery tubes to keep track of the total number of cells sorted.
Aspirate the supernatant, do not disturb the pelleted cells.
Centrifuge the cultures at 300 x g, °C, 5 minutes .
When ready to harvest, transfer the cultures to 50mL centrifuge tubes.
Allow the cultures to grow for an additional 2-3 days, or until the culture reaches a density of approximately 1.2M cells/mL.

Allow the cultures to grow for 3 days, then add an additional 10 mL of Freestyle 293 media and 1% penicillin-streptomycin to each culture flask ( 100 µL for 10 mL culture) for a total culture volume of 20 mL .

Plate the cells from each sorted Bin into a culture flask with 9 mL Freestyle 293 media (plus the 1 mL of resuspended cells). Should have 4 flasks labeled by bin number per sample sorted. Add 1% penicillin-streptomycin to each culture ( 100 µL for 10 mL culture).
Flash Freeze the pellets in labeled 50mL centrifuge tubes in LN2 for 00:00:30 , then store at -20 °C until ready to extract genomic DNA from cells.

For a typical staining experiment for single variants, you will need WT, any variants of interest, and a negative control without FIX. If you are staining for carboxylation with either GMA-001 or ab3570, you will need 2 WT samples, where one will be treated with warfarin.
The same controls are needed when staining libraries, except you will plate 2 flasks of library for each experiment.
Per control or single variant, you will plate 10 mL of cells at 3 x 10 5 cells/mL.
Per library, you will plate 2 flasks of 30 mL of cells at 3 x 10 5 cells/mL.
For library staining, split all media in 4 mL aliquots across 16 flow tubes. The last two tubes per flask will be closer to 2.5 mL - 3 mL each.

For libraries, do this for all flow tubes, but take the last 4 tubes with 2.5 mL - 3 mL media and split them equally across the remaining 12 tubes. This makes it easier to mix antibodies and stain in a single tube and also matches the limit of cells I can spin in my centrifuge. It’s not strictly necessary, but I find it helps.
creator: Sarah Heidl
